reasoning: The linear trend of reserve balances (WRBWFRBL) had been downwards due to Quantitative Tightening (QT), yielding a naively low base rate for an increase (around 47% from trend analysis). However, web searches confirm that the Fed officially ended QT on December 1, 2025, earlier than initially expected. Furthermore, due to liquidity concerns, the Fed initiated "Reserve Management Purchases" (expanding the balance sheet via T-bill purchases) starting around December 12, effectively restarting a form of QE to maintain ample reserves. The baseline value represented by Dec 21 corresponds to the Dec 17 data period, which captures the mid-December corporate tax deadline that typically drains reserves into the Treasury General Account (TGA). Consequently, the reversal of this tax drain as the Treasury spends, combined with the new active T-bill purchases and seasonal year-end liquidity, heavily biases reserves to the upside in the near term. The probabilities strongly favor an increase, but strictly following the prompt's instruction, the estimates are progressively pulled closer to 0.5 for the more distant horizon dates to reflect increasing temporal uncertainty.
